_comment: Key illustrated events for Chapter 14 — Farewell, Teacher (quiet/short, emotional). Verified spine: Dadoji Konddev died ~1647 and Shivaji (~17) took full charge of the Pune jagir, using his Sanskrit Rajmudra seal (Devanagari, not Persian; 'for the welfare of the people'). Deathbed dialogue is an imagined representative dramatisation (flagged). Dadoji kept neutral and role-focused per sensitivity note. 'anchor' is an EXACT substring of chapters/ch14.md. 'refs' are entity slugs from reference/index.json.

events.title: The pen he could no longer hold
events.anchor: could no longer hold the pen
events.scene_prompt: A quiet, tender domestic interior in the Lal Mahal: the elderly administrator Dadoji Konddev, now frail and white-haired and ill, sits propped among cushions, his trembling old hand having just set down a reed pen over an open ledger/account scroll he is too weak to write in. Teenage Shivaji sits close beside him, attentive and gently concerned, ready to read the accounts aloud for him. Soft warm indoor light, hushed and intimate, the sadness of a strong man grown weak. Dadoji matches his reference but visibly older, frailer and unwell. Shivaji has his thin moustache and no beard, matching his reference.

events.title: Govern well
events.anchor: Shivaji bent down to hear him
events.scene_prompt: The emotional heart of the chapter, handled gently and without anything gruesome: the dying old teacher Dadoji Konddev lies among cushions in a softly lamplit room of the Lal Mahal, very frail and near the end, and reaches up to speak his last counsel ('Govern well') as teenage Shivaji bends low over him, holding the old man's hand, his own face full of grief and love and resolve, leaning in to catch the faint words. Profoundly tender, quiet, reverent — a deathbed farewell between teacher and student. Warm low lamplight, soft shadows. NOT gory or frightening, just sad and loving. Dadoji matches his reference but aged and frail. Shivaji has his thin moustache and no beard, matching his reference.

events.title: A different house
events.scene_prompt: A quiet scene of dignified grief: teenage Shivaji and his mother Jijabai stand together in mourning in the Lal Mahal after the teacher's death — Shivaji grieving openly but with composure, Jijabai beside him with her back straight and her sorrow held quiet behind steady eyes, a comforting hand perhaps on her son's shoulder. Subdued, soft, sorrowful light; an empty cushioned seat or a closed ledger in the background hinting at the absence left behind. Restrained and moving, no bodies shown. Jijabai matches her reference (now a little older); Shivaji has his thin moustache and no beard, matching his reference.

events.title: His own seal
events.anchor: He pressed it into the warm wax and lifted it away
events.scene_prompt: A quietly momentous close-up moment by lamplight: teenage Shivaji's hand presses his new octagonal royal seal-stamp (the Rajmudra) down into a blob of warm red wax on a folded cream letter / official order on the desk, lifting it to reveal the fresh round seal impression — the first order issued under his own authority. Shivaji's face above is solemn, proud and resolute, freshly grown into his role. Intimate, significant, dignified mood — a small act of great defiance. Warm lamp glow, focus on the seal, the stamp and the letter. The seal matches the Rajmudra reference (ornate octagonal, decorative engraving only, no readable text). Shivaji has his thin moustache and no beard, matching his reference.
